title: Accessibility-first engineering is not a checklist. It changes the whole product.
excerpt: Working on accessibility tools changed how I think about latency, interface friction, and what ‘done’ should mean in software.
approvalNotes: Natural and strong. Could become featured if Marco wants accessibility to stay central to the portfolio narrative.
recruiterSignal: Builds with real user constraints in mind
Understands accessibility as an engineering requirement
Brings product empathy without losing technical rigor
summary: Accessibility work has changed the way I build software in general. Once you are designing for users who depend on keyboard support, screen readers, low-friction flows, and clear feedback, you stop treating accessibility as a final pass and start treating it as part of the system design from the beginning.

sections.heading: What changed for me
content: Before I worked deeply on accessibility-focused projects, it was easy to think about accessibility as a quality bar you verify near the end. In practice, that mindset breaks down fast.
If a user depends on spoken feedback or keyboard navigation, then latency, layout order, focus management, and error messaging are not polish. They are the product.

sections.heading: The engineering impact
bullets: You think harder about interaction flow because extra steps carry real cost
You notice ambiguous UI language faster because it blocks comprehension
You care more about failure states because users cannot be abandoned in them
You design for consistency because surprises are expensive

sections.heading: Why recruiters and teams should care
content: Accessibility work is a forcing function for better engineering judgment. It makes you confront the difference between software that merely functions and software that can actually be used with confidence.
Teams benefit from that thinking well beyond accessibility-specific products. Cleaner interaction models, clearer state transitions, and better feedback loops improve the experience for everyone.

sections.heading: How this shows up in my work
bullets: I default to keyboard-friendly, lower-friction interaction patterns
I think about response time and clarity together
I care about whether an interface is understandable under imperfect conditions
I treat usability constraints as architecture inputs, not afterthoughts

sections.heading: Bottom line
content: Accessibility has made me a sharper engineer because it removes the illusion that surface-level success is enough. The software either helps the user accomplish something clearly and reliably, or it does not.
That standard is useful on every team, whether the product is explicitly accessibility-focused or not.
